Arctic Coast Trading Post Sign.description: Large, weathered sign reading 'ARCTIC COAST TRADING POST' with 'America's Most Northern Grocer' and 'UTQIAGVIK, ALASKA' beneath it, mounted on a gray building; visible in the background of the town scene.

Reindeer Herd.description: A group of reindeer with large antlers moving in a line across a golden-brown tundra under a clear blue sky, visually representing the 'caribou' mentioned by Gordon Brower as a vital food resource.

Fishing Boat.description: Small, dark-colored fishing boat with two masts and fishing gear, moving across calm waters near forested hills under an overcast sky; not visible in this segment, but contextually relevant to the coastal setting.

All-Terrain Vehicles.description: Two gray all-terrain vehicles parked in a grassy area, one with a visible front windshield and handlebars, suggesting local use for transport or recreation; visible in the background of the town scene.

Flashlight.description: Small, handheld flashlight held by Gordon Brower, emitting a bright beam that illuminates the dark, icy tunnel walls and floor.

Rope for Descent.description: Thick, dark rope attached to the tunnel ceiling, used by Gordon Brower to descend into the underground passage, with visible wear and fraying.

Wooden Ladder in Ice Tunnel.description: Rusty, weathered wooden ladder fixed within the icy tunnel, used for descent and ascent, with visible ice and dirt clinging to its rungs.

Underground Ice Tunnel.description: A narrow, dark, icy underground passage with rough, frozen walls and a wooden ladder, accessed via a rope and flashlight. Gordon Brower identifies it as an 'ice cellar' used by his family for generations to store whale meat for the community, but it is now experiencing thawing due to permafrost degradation.

raw_segments: The segment opens with an aerial view of a dense, lush green forest, accompanied by somber, atmospheric background music. The scene transitions to a close-up of Tom Douglas, identified by on-screen text as from the Cold Regions Research & Engineering Laboratory, standing amidst overgrown vegetation. He speaks in a calm, clear voice, describing the dramatic and rapid changes in the landscape occurring in a matter of years, not decades, since 2018. The PBS NOVA logo remains consistently displayed in the top left corner. An Earth satellite view then zooms into the Arctic region, highlighting Utqiagvik, Alaska, with a red dot, as a narrator explains that this rapid thaw is affecting human settlements like Utqiagvik, the most northern city in the United States. An aerial shot of the coastal town, with its scattered buildings and proximity to the sea, follows. The narrator continues, stating that this entire community sits on top of the Alaskan permafrost, and locals call it 'the top of the world.' The camera then focuses on a weathered wooden signpost with directional arrows pointing to global cities like Paris and Greenland. The scene shifts to a rocky shoreline with waves crashing, where Gordon Brower, identified by on-screen text as a whaling captain, stands in a bright blue jacket. He speaks in a friendly, slightly gravelly voice, gesturing toward the ocean and humorously pointing out directions to Greenland, Canada, and Russia. The narrator then identifies him as a native Alaskan Inupaq whaling captain. The segment concludes with a view of a beached, green-hulled boat and a large whalebone arch on the shore, symbolizing the town’s maritime heritage, as the background music continues.

The segment opens with a close-up of a weathered, gray wooden boat frame resting on a beach next to a large whalebone arch, as a calm, slightly melancholic background music begins and a narrator states that people have lived in this region for thousands of years. The scene transitions to a carved wooden owl sculpture against a clear blue sky, then to a directional signpost labeled 'BARROW' pointing to distant cities, and a small cemetery with white crosses. A street sign for 'HERMAN ST' and 'AHKCOAK ST' is shown before Gordon Brower, a man in a bright blue jacket with an 'Utqiagvik Whaling Captain' patch and sunglasses, appears on a rocky shoreline. He speaks with a calm, reflective, slightly gravelly voice, explaining that communities like these are special, and while they are assimilated, they have brought their culture and ways to the future, allowing visitors to still see celebrations from 10,000 years ago. Dynamic ocean waves crash onto the shore, followed by an aerial view of the coastline, as the music continues. A second narrator then explains that Brower has been hunting in these waters for five decades, upholding an ancient Inupack tradition of living off the land and sea. Gordon Brower is next seen near a white building, continuing to speak about the community's self-sufficiency, noting they don't have big stores like Walmart or McDonald's, but rely on traditional hunting. A large sign for 'ARCTIC COAST TRADING POST' in Utqiagvik, Alaska, is shown, followed by two all-terrain vehicles parked in a grassy area. Brower emphasizes that the majority of their food resources are still hunted today, listing seals, whales, belugas, ducks, geese, caribou, and wolves. The video concludes with a fishing boat navigating calm waters near forested hills and a wide shot of a reindeer herd moving across a golden tundra, visually representing the caribou he mentioned. The PBS NOVA logo remains visible in the top left corner throughout.

The segment opens with a close-up of strips of dried fish hanging from a weathered wooden rack by the sea, with waves crashing in the background and a distant shoreline under an overcast sky, accompanied by a somber, atmospheric background score. A narrator states that these items "are still traded and used." The scene transitions to a rural coastal town, Utqiagvik, where a man, Gordon Brower, in a bright blue jacket with an 'Utqiagvik Whaling Captain' patch, exits a gray pickup truck. The narrator explains that "with little fresh food available in winter, generations of Native Alaskans have depended on cellars carved out of the permafrost." Gordon walks toward a small wooden shed, then descends into a narrow, icy underground passage using a rope and flashlight. Inside the subterranean space, lined with ice and dirt, Gordon's voice is heard, confirming, "We're in an ice cellar." He explains its generational use: "My folks used this ice cellar for years and years. This is where I put a whale and store it in trust for the community." The camera follows him as he explores the dark, damp tunnel, illuminated by his flashlight, showing close-ups of icy walls and a wooden ladder. However, the narrator interjects, "But thawing permafrost means this natural deep freeze isn't as cold as it used to be, as Brower discovered." Gordon then recounts his experience with the thawing cellar: "I had to checked on the meat and told my brothers, you know, we got to pull that meat out. It's greening, and we don't want that." He further explains his adaptation: "And I've resorted to pulling a whole whale out of there and putting it into walk-in freezers." He emerges back into the daylight outside the shed, as the sound of crashing waves intensifies. The scene cuts to a close-up of foamy waves washing onto a rocky shore, while the narrator concludes, "Thawing ice cellars aren't the only threat." The PBS NOVA logo remains in the top left corner throughout.

event_blocks.summary: Under an overcast sky, foamy ocean waves relentlessly crash onto a dark, pebbly shore, eroding the coastline as a narrator explains that melting sea ice, once a protective barrier, exacerbates the threat of rising temperatures to communities like Utqiagvik. Sandbags are piled near the water's edge, indicating efforts to combat erosion, while Gordon Brower, a whaling captain, passionately describes how storm surges dramatically undercut permafrost-rich banks, leading to land collapse and exposing root systems and cracked earth. A damaged utility pole leans precariously, highlighting infrastructure risks in Utqiagvik, a town characterized by scattered buildings, wetlands, and a helicopter landing pad marked 'BROWER', all under a gray sky. Brower emphasizes these are local efforts to save the town from storm surges, as the narrator mentions a retreating coastline and warming ice cellars. Meanwhile, scientists like Tom Douglas explore underground permafrost tunnels, such as the Fox Permafrost Tunnel, revealing deep cracks in the frozen earth and vast quantities of organic matter, including fossilized mammoth bone and upside-down sedges preserved for thousands of years. A poster with geological information is visible in the tunnel, where Douglas descends deeper into the permafrost, a process only possible in a few locations on Earth, as a female scientist notes that between 30 and 70% of near-surface permafrost could be lost across the Arctic in the coming decades, with implications for the entire planet due to potential methane release.

Eroded Cliff.description: Steep, collapsing cliff face with exposed dark soil, tangled roots, and grassy top, showing severe coastal erosion, with waves crashing at its base.

Helicopter Landing Pad.description: Blue and yellow landing pad marked 'BROWER' on a grassy field, with a helicopter visible in one shot, suggesting local transportation and emergency access.

Utqiagvik.description: Coastal town in Alaska, shown in an aerial view with scattered buildings, industrial structures, a helicopter landing pad marked 'BROWER,' and proximity to the sea, under an overcast sky; depicted as a small, rural settlement with wooden houses, sheds, vehicles, and wetlands. The narrator explicitly names it as a community trying to protect their homes.

Stormy Seawall.description: Concrete seawall with waves crashing against it during a storm, with a distant city skyline; here, the focus is on the eroded shoreline and collapsing land, not a seawall, but the concept of coastal defense is implied by the sandbags and damaged infrastructure.

Wetlands Near Town.description: Shallow, water-filled depressions and marshy areas surrounding the town, visible in aerial shots, suggesting a low-lying, vulnerable landscape prone to flooding.

Collapsed Landmass.description: Large section of land that has broken away from the cliff, exposing layers of soil and roots, with grassy top and direct exposure to ocean waves, indicating recent or ongoing erosion.

Sandbags.description: Large white sandbags piled near the shoreline, some torn and filled with sand, used as a temporary barrier against wave erosion, visible in multiple shots.

Eroded Shoreline.description: Dark, pebbly beach with waves continuously washing over it, showing signs of erosion and instability, with visible cracks and collapsed earth near the water’s edge.

Damaged Utility Pole.description: Leaning wooden utility pole with wires, situated near a road and houses, indicating infrastructure damage due to land instability.

Gordon Brower.description: Man in a bright blue jacket with a circular patch reading 'Utqiagvik Whaling Captain,' wearing sunglasses, standing on a rocky shoreline and gesturing toward the ocean. He speaks with a distinct, passionate voice about coastal erosion and infrastructure risks, stating that 'Storm surge is pretty dramatic' and 'it's gonna wreak havoc on your coastline here,' explaining how 'permafrost rich' banks are undercut, and declaring, 'These are all our local efforts to stop the storm surge' and 'This is our way of trying to save the town.'

PBS NOVA Logo.description: White circular logo with 'PBS | NOVA' text, consistently displayed in the top left corner throughout the segment, overlaying all scenes.

Yellow Sign in Tunnel.description: Yellow rectangular sign attached to the rocky wall of an underground tunnel, reading '4300 ft. 100 year old permafrost - T. D. Smith,' indicating scientific research and depth of permafrost study; here, it is not visible, but the context suggests a research-focused environment.

Person in Hard Hat.description: An excited male researcher or explorer, wearing a white hard hat with a red stripe and a black jacket, holding a flashlight, who walks through the cave, identifies a fossil as a "horn" from an "extinct longhorn step bison" and then a "mammoth bone," and explains that delicate, root-like structures are "sedges" that were part of a geological event where a "block fell into a water feature that then froze" approximately "20 or so thousand years ago."

Forest Aerial View.description: Aerial shot of a dense, lush green forest with a mix of coniferous and deciduous trees, viewed from above as the camera glides over the treetops under natural daylight; here, it is a dense, green forest with a narrow dirt path cutting through the trees, shown amidst discussions of permafrost thaw's global implications.

River.description: Calm, greenish water with ripples and bubbles, reflecting surrounding trees, shown in multiple close-ups with methane bubbles rising to the surface; here, it is a wide, meandering river cutting through a tundra landscape, bordered by sparse vegetation and mountains, visually part of the Arctic landscape affected by permafrost thaw.

Underground Ice Tunnel.description: Referred to by a calm male narrator as "The Fox Permafrost Tunnel," a dimly lit, rocky underground cave with artificial lighting, which, despite not being icy, is a subterranean research environment revealing permafrost and vast quantities of organic matter.

Arctic Tundra.description: Vast, open grassy plain with low shrubs, yellow wildflowers, and a calm river, under a cloudy sky, shown in both ground-level and aerial views; here, it appears as a rugged, mountainous tundra with a winding river and sparse vegetation, visually representing the 'Arctic-wide problem' of permafrost thaw.

Rugged Terrain.description: Partially snow-dusted, rocky landscape with winding streams and sparse vegetation, shown in an aerial shot; here, it is a mountainous, rocky terrain with exposed sediment and icicles, indicating permafrost degradation, visually illustrating the 'big thaw' and its implications.

Fossilized Spiral Object.description: A large, curved tusk-like fossil protruding from the cave wall, initially identified by the explorer as a "horn" from an "extinct longhorn step bison" from "18 to 40,000 years ago," and then more specifically as a "mammoth bone."

Poster with Geological Information.description: A white paper poster affixed to the cave wall, containing images and text, which the explorer points to while discussing "sedges" and a geological event where a "block fell into a water feature that then froze" approximately "20 or so thousand years ago," suggesting it details related geological or archaeological findings.

Document with Crater Images.description: Paper with satellite images of craters, labeled with coordinates and Russian text such as 'Окно диаметром 100 м' (Window diameter 100 m) and 'Окно размером 81×57 м' (Window size 81×57 m), held by hands in a close-up shot; here, it is replaced by a poster affixed to the cave wall, containing images and text related to the cave's geological features.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of foamy ocean waves crashing onto a dark, pebbly shore under an overcast sky, establishing a cold, stormy coastal atmosphere, accompanied by the constant sound of crashing waves and wind. A male narrator explains that rising temperatures pose a threat to this community, as sea ice that used to protect the shore from storms has begun to melt. An aerial shot then reveals a narrow, eroded land bridge or causeway separating the ocean from a calm inland body of water, with vehicles visible, emphasizing human presence in a vulnerable landscape. The camera returns to the shore, showing waves relentlessly eroding the coastline, followed by a shot of large white sandbags piled near the water’s edge, indicating efforts to combat erosion. Gordon Brower, wearing a bright blue jacket with an 'Utqiagvik Whaling Captain' patch and sunglasses, appears on screen, gesturing toward the ocean. With a distinct, passionate voice, he describes how "Storm surge is pretty dramatic" and "it's gonna wreak havoc on your coastline here," explaining that when the water reaches the edge, it undercuts the "permafrost rich" banks. The scene shifts to a dramatic cliffside where the land has collapsed, exposing dark, wet soil and tangled roots, with waves crashing below, a visual testament to the erosion. The narrator adds that exposed by the storms, permafrost is thawing and crumbling away, as a close-up reveals the exposed root system and cracked earth. A damaged utility pole leans precariously near a road with houses in the background, underscoring infrastructure risks. The narrator states that communities like Utqiagvik are trying to protect their homes, as aerial views capture a helicopter landing pad marked 'BROWER' on a grassy field, and a wider shot of the town of Utqiagvik, with scattered buildings, a large body of water, and wetlands, all under a gray, cloudy sky, reinforcing the remote, fragile nature of the community. Gordon Brower's voice returns, declaring, "These are all our local efforts to stop the storm surge," and "This is our way of trying to save the town," as the narrator concludes by mentioning a retreating coastline and warming ice cellars. The PBS NOVA logo remains visible in the top left corner throughout.

The segment opens with the white circular PBS NOVA logo consistently displayed in the top left corner, as a male narrator explains that "communities are hit twice by thawing permafrost." An aerial view of a vast wetland landscape near a coastal town, featuring scattered pools, grassy terrain, and a research facility with satellite dishes under an overcast sky, transitions to a close-up of a snow-covered, textured surface, possibly permafrost, with deep cracks, suggesting instability. The narrator emphasizes, "the big thaw is an Arctic-wide problem," as a sweeping aerial shot reveals a rugged, mountainous tundra with a winding river cutting through sparse vegetation under a bright blue sky. A female scientist then elaborates, stating that "through the next decades and century, we expect anywhere across the Arctic, between 30 and 70% of near-surface permafrost will be lost," attributing the range to scientific uncertainty and future warming, while the visuals show a close-up of a rocky, icy cliff face with icicles and exposed sediment, indicating permafrost degradation. The scene shifts to a dense, green forest from above with a narrow dirt path, then to a dimly lit underground tunnel with rough, icy walls and artificial lights. The narrator returns, noting "Arctic communities are facing the immediate effects now," and that "scientists are concerned this loss has implications for the entire planet." A person in a hard hat, later identified as Tom Douglas, walks through this tunnel, which is lined with pipes and features a yellow sign reading '4300 ft. 100 year old permafrost - T. D. Smith,' highlighting the scientific exploration. The narrator then poses critical questions: "So why is permafrost thaw so dangerous? And what is the link to methane?" accompanied by dramatic music and subtle environmental sounds. The segment concludes with the narrator introducing Tom Douglas near Fox, interior Alaska, who is "about to do something only possible in a few places on Earth: walk deep down" into the permafrost, as the person in the hard hat continues their descent.

The video opens with a wide shot of a dimly lit, expansive underground cave, illuminated by strategically placed warm lights that reveal rugged, layered rock formations and a sandy floor. A calm male narrator introduces the scene, stating that the segment delves into the "permafrost itself" and that "The Fox Permafrost Tunnel reveals there's more to permafrost than frozen earth." Amidst ambient cave sounds, a person wearing a white hard hat with a red stripe and a black jacket, identified as a researcher or explorer, walks through the cave, holding a flashlight and examining the surroundings. The camera follows them as they move deeper, pausing as the explorer, speaking with an excited tone, inspects a large, fossilized tusk embedded in the wall. He identifies it first as "a horn" from an "extinct longhorn step bison" from "18 to 40,000 years ago," then excitedly clarifies, "this is a mammoth bone right here," pointing out the "piece of it sticking out." The narrator then adds that "the tunnel reveals that permafrost contains vast quantities of organic matter." The explorer then points to a section of the cave wall where a poster with images and text is affixed, possibly detailing geological or archaeological findings. The scene shifts to a close-up of the explorer’s hand gently touching delicate, root-like structures hanging from the ceiling, which he identifies as "sedges, kind of like grass," noting they are "green" with "chlorophyll" and "upside down." He explains that "this block fell into a water feature that then froze," likely "20 or so thousand years ago," as the camera pans to reveal a narrow, dark crevice in the rock with visible moisture, all within the warm, artificial lighting emphasizing the textures of the cave walls and the atmosphere of scientific exploration and discovery.

event_blocks.summary: Sunlight filters through a serene forest, illuminating a leaf-strewn path as a narrator explains that all life, like the delicate green seedling emerging from dark soil, contains carbon and is part of the vital carbon cycle. The process of decomposition, illustrated by decaying lettuce and moldy strawberries with accompanying bubbling and gurgling sounds, releases some carbon back into the atmosphere, but in the freezing Arctic, decomposition happens slowly, allowing vast amounts of organic matter and its carbon to become permafrost, frozen in time. Within a dimly lit underground tunnel, a person in a hard hat examines the rough, rocky walls, revealing hanging root-like structures and a fossilized tusk-like object, while an expert notes that permafrost contains about 1,400 billion metric tons of carbon. The scene shifts to an aerial view of Falmouth, Massachusetts, 3,000 miles from the ice tunnel, and then to a lush backyard where Arctic ecologist Susan Natali collects permafrost core samples, like one labeled 'NORTH SHORE INTERIOR' with yellow tape, in a small shed. These dark, carbon-rich samples, collected across Alaska, signify high carbon content. An animated Earth from space shows that as permafrost warms and thaws, its carbon restarts the carbon cycle, becoming available for microbes to break down, releasing carbon dioxide or methane, both greenhouse gases that trap heat and warm the planet. Hanna Lee explains in a laboratory that permafrost is permanently frozen ground, its carbon stable but vulnerable upon thawing, leading to a dark, eroded hole in the ground. Carbon dioxide emissions from the North Alaskan wilderness have spiked over 70% since the mid-70s, as seen in aerial views of a massive glacier and a road along a fjord. The impact of methane, a less widely known greenhouse gas escaping from the permafrost with distinct bubbling sounds underwater, is then highlighted, with the woman in the green jacket explaining its potency, being about 30 times more powerful than carbon dioxide in trapping heat.

Fossilized Spiral Object.description: Large, curved tusk-like fossil protruding from the cave wall, likely from a prehistoric mammal such as a mammoth or mastodon, embedded in the rocky surface.

Poster with Geological Information.description: White paper poster affixed to the cave wall, containing images and text, likely detailing geological or archaeological findings, illuminated by a hanging lantern and a flashlight, suggesting a research or educational display within the cave.

Broken Ceramic Shard.description: Fragile, off-white ceramic fragment with visible cracks and chipped edges, lying on the dusty floor of the underground tunnel, suggesting past human activity or archaeological significance.

Flashlight.description: Small, handheld flashlight held by the person in the hard hat, emitting a bright beam that illuminates the dark, rocky tunnel walls and floor, revealing textures and formations.

Person in Hard Hat.description: An individual wearing a white hard hat with a red stripe and a black jacket (Cold Regions Research & Engineering Laboratory logo), holding a flashlight, walking through a dimly lit underground cave, examining rock formations and fossilized remains, and interacting with mineral or root-like structures hanging from the ceiling, speaking with a male voice about the smell of 'ancient bacteria and carbon being oxidized' and the ultimate fate of permafrost carbon.

Underground Ice Tunnel.description: Narrow, dimly lit, rocky underground passage with rough, textured walls and artificial lighting, not literally icy, but a subterranean research environment where 'permafrost carbon' is smelled and its fate is being investigated, featuring hanging root-like structures and a small pool of water.

PBS NOVA Logo.description: White circular logo with 'PBS | NOVA' text, consistently displayed in the top left corner throughout the segment, overlaying all scenes.

Yellow Tape.description: Yellow tape with handwritten text 'NORTH SHORE INTERIOR' affixed to a rock sample wrapped in foil, indicating its origin and use in geological research.

Woman in Green Jacket.description: Woman with shoulder-length gray hair, wearing a green jacket over a black top and a silver pendant necklace, standing in front of a bookshelf-lined background, speaking clearly and articulately about methane's importance and its potency in trapping heat.

Rock Sample.description: Geological specimen, identified as a permafrost core, wrapped in aluminum foil and labeled with yellow tape reading 'NORTH SHORE INTERIOR.' The Woman in Green Jacket describes it as dark, irregularly shaped, with rough, layered texture and embedded organic material (moss, twigs), indicating it contains a lot of carbon from permafrost environments.

Shed.description: Small, light green wooden shed with a shingled roof, located in a grassy backyard, containing tools and rock samples, serving as a field research station or storage area.

Earth Satellite View.description: Rotating view of Earth from space, showing continents, oceans, and a bright light source, with a glowing teal ring and an inner red ring filled with dark particles appearing around the planet, while a male narrator discusses the increase in greenhouse gases from 2,000 to over 3,000 gigatons and a 2-degree Fahrenheit temperature rise.

Industrial Cityscape.description: Aerial view of a city with tall residential buildings, industrial cooling towers emitting white steam, construction cranes, and a hazy, overcast sky, visually representing the 'industrialization' and 'human-made greenhouse gas emissions' discussed by the narrator.

Rugged Terrain.description: Exposed, eroded permafrost landscape with dark, crumbling soil, green moss, grass, and a fallen log, showing signs of environmental degradation and thawing ground, captured in a close-up shot, visually representing the permafrost discussed by the narrators.

Glacier Landscape.description: Vast, snow-covered glacier with rugged mountain peaks under a bright blue sky with scattered clouds, shown in an aerial view with textured ice and surrounding green hills, appearing as a visual representation of the broader climate impacts discussed by the male voiceover.

Hanna Lee.description: Woman with long black hair, wearing glasses, a black top, and a gray patterned scarf with a blue accent, speaking clearly and articulately in a laboratory setting about permafrost being permanently frozen ground and the vulnerability of its carbon as it thaws.

River.description: Calm, greenish water with ripples and distinct bubbling sounds rising to the surface, reflecting surrounding trees, shown in an underwater close-up, explicitly identified as methane release by a male voiceover and further explained by a female speaker as a potent greenhouse gas.

Aerial Road Along Fjord.description: Winding road along a fjord, flanked by autumn-colored forests and calm water, with vehicles moving along the road, captured from an aerial perspective, providing a scenic view amidst the discussion of environmental changes.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a serene, sun-dappled forest scene, where sunlight filters through lush green trees, casting long shadows on a leaf-strewn path, evoking natural renewal. A calm, authoritative male narrator begins, explaining that "This ancient organic matter, like all life on our planet, contains carbon," accompanied by subtle, atmospheric music. The scene transitions to a close-up of a delicate green seedling emerging from dark soil, symbolizing growth, as the narrator continues, "and is part of a vital Earth system called the carbon cycle." Shots of decaying lettuce leaves, their edges brown and curled, and rotting strawberries covered in white mold and darkened by decay, illustrate decomposition. The narrator explains, "As they grow, plants absorb carbon dioxide," followed by "When they die, they, or the animals that have eaten them, decompose," with distinct bubbling and gurgling sounds accompanying the visuals of decay. He adds that this process releases "some of this carbon back into the atmosphere." The setting abruptly changes to a dimly lit, narrow underground tunnel with rough, rocky walls illuminated by sparse artificial lights, creating an eerie, subterranean atmosphere. The narrator clarifies, "But in the freezing Arctic, decomposition happens slowly." The camera pans across the tunnel, revealing hanging root-like structures and a small pool of water reflecting the light. He continues, "So over millennia, a huge amount of organic matter became permafrost before it could decompose. Its carbon frozen in time." A person in a white hard hat with a red stripe and a black jacket, holding a flashlight, walks through the tunnel, examining the environment. The camera focuses on a fossilized tusk-like object embedded in the rock wall. The person continues to explore, shining the flashlight on the walls, revealing more textures and formations, including a poster with geological information affixed to the wall, illuminated by a lantern. A second male speaker, an expert, then adds, "In the late 90s into the 2000s, people start to look at the stocks of carbon in permafrost and it's, it's a lot. It's about 1,400 billion metric tons," as the sequence ends with a close-up of a broken ceramic shard on the tunnel floor, hinting at human or archaeological presence. The white circular PBS NOVA logo is consistently displayed in the top left corner throughout.

The segment opens in a dimly lit, narrow underground tunnel with rough, rocky walls illuminated by warm artificial lights, where a male voice explains that the carbon present is almost twice as much as currently in our atmosphere. A man in a white hard hat with a red stripe and a black jacket, bearing the Cold Regions Research & Engineering Laboratory logo, walks through the tunnel, holding a flashlight and gesturing, describing a distinct smell as "ancient bacteria and carbon being oxidized." He elaborates on the smell, comparing it to "French cheese to barnyard" or a "late fall wet leaf," confirming it's "permafrost carbon being oxidized," as the camera shows hanging root-like structures, a small pool of water, and a white, mineralized vein in the rock. He then poses the central question: "what's its ultimate fate?" for the carbon smelled throughout the tunnel walls. A new male narrator's voice takes over, asking how much of this carbon will end up in the atmosphere and how fast, accompanied by dramatic, suspenseful music. The scene transitions to an aerial view of a coastal town with boats in a harbor, as a voiceover identifies the location as "Falmouth, Massachusetts," highlighting that it's "3,000 miles from the ice tunnel." The setting then shifts to a lush green backyard where a woman in a green jacket enters a small shed. The narrator introduces her as "Arctic ecologist Susan Natali," who investigates permafrost samples to understand what happens when it thaws. Inside the shed, she handles rock samples wrapped in foil, one clearly labeled 'NORTH SHORE INTERIOR' with yellow tape, as the music continues.

The segment opens in a small, light green wooden shed, serving as a field research station, where a Woman in Green Jacket with shoulder-length gray hair and purple gloves carefully handles a geological specimen. She identifies it as a permafrost core, explaining in her voice that these samples, like the one wrapped in aluminum foil labeled 'NORTH SHORE INTERIOR' with yellow tape, were collected from various locations across Alaska. She picks up a dark, irregularly shaped specimen, revealing its rough, layered texture and embedded organic material like moss and twigs, and states that its dark color signifies a high carbon content. The scene then transitions to a breathtaking animated sequence of Earth from space, with a white circular PBS NOVA logo consistently displayed in the top left. A narrator's voice explains that as permafrost warms, its carbon thaws, restarting the carbon cycle. The Woman in Green Jacket's voice returns, detailing how this carbon becomes available for microbes to break down for energy, releasing carbon dioxide or methane in the process. The animation continues, showing a sunrise over Earth’s curved horizon, gliding over blue oceans and swirling clouds, gradually zooming towards the Arctic region, specifically highlighting Utqiagvik with a blue circular overlay. Another narrator's voice clarifies that carbon dioxide and methane are both greenhouse gases. As Earth's surface absorbs energy from the sun, it radiates some back out as heat. In the atmosphere, these greenhouse gases absorb this heat, radiating part of its energy back at Earth, thus heating up our planet. The Woman in Green Jacket's voice concludes, stating that greenhouse gases are a concern because...

The segment begins with a Woman in Green Jacket, wearing a silver pendant necklace and shoulder-length gray hair, speaking directly to the camera from a wooden shed with bookshelves, explaining that while certain elements trap heat and are helpful in making Earth habitable, too much in the atmosphere is now making it an uninhabitable or less habitable planet. As she concludes, the scene transitions to a rotating Earth satellite view from space, showing continents and oceans illuminated by a bright light source, accompanied by dramatic background music. A glowing teal ring appears around Earth, followed by an inner red ring filled with dark, particle-like dots, symbolizing layers of atmospheric or environmental data. A male narrator explains that in the mid-18th century, there were over 2,000 gigatons of naturally occurring carbon-based greenhouse gases in the atmosphere, but with industrialization, human-made emissions began to add to this, rising to over 3,000 gigatons by 2019. He adds that over the last century and a half, Earth's average temperature increased around 2 degrees Fahrenheit. The scene then shifts to an industrial cityscape with tall buildings, cooling towers emitting white steam, and construction cranes, visually reinforcing the impact of industrialization and human-made emissions. The narrator continues, stating that scientists agree human emissions caused this warming, but recently they've become concerned about greenhouse gases being released by permafrost. The video then cuts to a close-up of a collapsing permafrost landscape, showing exposed dark soil, eroded edges, a fallen log, and green moss and grass, directly illustrating the narrator's concern as the background music intensifies with a subtle ominous hum. The PBS NOVA logo remains consistently in the top left corner throughout.

The segment begins with Hanna Lee, identified by on-screen text as from the Norwegian University of Science and Technology, speaking directly to the camera in a laboratory setting. She wears glasses, a black top, and a gray patterned scarf, explaining in a clear, articulate voice that permafrost is permanently frozen ground and its carbon is stable, but becomes vulnerable as it thaws. A subtle, continuous hum underlies her speech. The scene transitions to a close-up of a tundra landscape with delicate white flowers, followed by a shot of a dark, eroded hole in the ground, indicative of permafrost thaw. A male voiceover then begins, discussing how carbon dioxide emissions from the North Alaskan wilderness have spiked by over 70% since the mid-70s, as the visuals shift to an expansive aerial view of a massive glacier, its icy surface textured with crevasses, bordered by green hills. This is followed by another aerial shot of a winding road along a fjord, flanked by autumn-colored forests and calm water, with vehicles moving. The male voiceover continues, highlighting that while carbon dioxide is well-known, the impact of another greenhouse gas coming out of the permafrost is less widely known. The scene then moves to an underwater view of murky green water with distinct bubbling sounds, which the male voiceover explicitly identifies as methane escaping. The segment concludes with a woman in a green jacket over a black top, wearing a silver pendant, speaking in front of a bookshelf-lined background. She explains in a clear, articulate voice that methane is really important because it's much more potent, about 30 times more powerful than carbon, in its ability to trap heat.

event_blocks.summary: An Earth satellite view reveals the planet's curvature and atmosphere against space, underscored by a resonant hum and a male voice stating "dioxide," before transitioning to a stormy seawall where massive waves crash violently, accompanied by the sounds of crashing waves and strong wind, as the narrator explains that "carbon dioxide lasts centuries or longer in our atmosphere." The scene shifts to a wildfire scene with intense orange flames consuming trees and thick smoke, marked by the crackling and roaring of fire, as the narrator notes that "methane only lasts around 12 years." Industrial cityscape visuals follow, with smokestacks releasing dark plumes and industrial hissing and machinery sounds, while the narrator emphasizes methane as "a far more potent greenhouse gas," adding that "any large-scale increases in methane emissions have climate scientists seriously concerned." An aerial view of an agricultural field and a close-up of an eroded cliff with icicles and snow suggest permafrost thaw, as the narrator explains that "more than half of methane emissions come from human sources like fossil fuels and agriculture," which are "sources well understood by climate experts," but expresses that "scientists are increasingly worried about methane emissions from permafrost," with the sound of dripping water reinforcing the visual, concluding, "they don't know how much methane the permafrost is releasing. And that's a big problem." Finally, a woman in a green jacket, positioned before a bookshelf, explains that "in order to control our temperature, we have a certain amount of carbon that humans can release, that's our carbon budget," before the video cuts to a congested highway and an aerial view of a city in smog, with the background hum fading and city traffic sounds becoming prominent.

event_blocks.summary: An orchestral score accompanies an aerial view of Paris, obscured by smog, as a narrator explains the 2015 International Paris Agreement's goal to limit global warming to 1.5 degrees Celsius, noting that humans can release a maximum of 460 gigatons more carbon dioxide. Cracked, dry earth with shallow puddles emphasizes environmental degradation, while a stylized animation of Earth from space, encircled by glowing red and teal rings filled with particles, symbolizes atmospheric pollution. The narrator cautions that recent climate calculations rely on incomplete computer models, and a female speaker adds that many Earth system models do not account for CO2 and methane emissions from permafrost. A satellite view of a massive hurricane over the Atlantic highlights extreme weather, before the Earth animation returns, now with the red ring filled with glowing particles, as the narrator concludes that recent carbon budgets have started to include permafrost carbon, with the PBS | NOVA logo visible. The narrative shifts to a rotating satellite view of Earth, where a narrator discusses scientists underestimating carbon release from the warming Arctic, making temperature goals harder to meet. An aerial view of a smog-shrouded city highlights urban air quality issues. A Woman in a Green Jacket explains that current greenhouse gas targets are incorrect due to not accounting for potential permafrost emissions of methane and carbon dioxide. An industrial cityscape with a tall smokestack emitting smoke emphasizes pollution, followed by a view of Earth from space. The narrator returns, stressing the critical importance of understanding thawing permafrost dynamics for predicting climate future and questioning the amount and potential increase of methane emissions from permafrost. The scene transitions to a vast, snow-covered Arctic tundra, where the sounds of dogs barking and howling emerge, accompanying a Dog Sled Team pulling a sled across the icy plain. Commands like "Good dog! Stay ahead! Come by!" are heard as the team, identified as being in Fairbanks, interior Alaska, moves through the forested tundra with Ecologist Katie Walter Anthony. The dogs are energetic, and the sled glides over the snow as the rider enjoys the ride.

event_blocks.summary: An aerial view of a snowy forest shows a dog sled team moving through rugged terrain, accompanied by dog barks and exclamations of "Woah!" and "Come on, piggy, come on!". The narrator introduces Katey Walter Anthony as a researcher of "Easy Lake," part of a permafrost region releasing methane, depicted in a vast, frozen landscape under a twilight sky. Suspenseful music builds as Katey, in a red puffer jacket, holds a flaming torch on a snowbank beside the frozen lake, while a person in a dark jacket, holding a red pole, prepares to ignite escaping gas. The person in the dark jacket explains the ignition procedure, and after confirming readiness, a striking sound, followed by a whoosh and hiss, signals the ignition. The torch is playfully thrown, resulting in a loud whoosh and explosion that knocks the person backward into the snow with a splash/thud, eliciting laughter and concern about being on fire. The torch continues to burn as the music becomes ambient. An underwater scene beneath the ice reveals a dark, rocky riverbed with light filtering through, as the narrator explains methane originates from decomposing organic matter in thawing permafrost. The scene transitions to a misty, thawing tundra with pooling water and emerging vegetation, indicating permafrost degradation, followed by an aerial view of a patchwork tundra with numerous small lakes and ponds, as the narrator describes permafrost thaw generating new lakes and ground slumping. Katey Walter Anthony explains that once formed, these lakes cannot be stopped and their heat causes rapid thawing, leading to methane release and a positive feedback cycle of further thawing and warming, which is not fully accounted for in climate models. The segment concludes with Hanna Lee in a laboratory setting, beginning to explain the release of greenhouse gases as permafrost thaws, with the PBS NOVA logo consistently visible.

event_blocks.summary: The segment opens with a time-lapse of a mountainous landscape at sunrise or sunset, illuminated by golden rays piercing dark clouds, with the PBS NOVA logo visible. Ominous background music begins as the scene transitions to an aerial view above a dense sea of clouds, followed by a stylized animated Earth from space, encircled by glowing rings, symbolizing atmospheric layers or pollution. Hanna Lee explains that "methane will be released back to the atmosphere much faster. Warming is causing more warming." The perspective shifts to a high-altitude view of a vast, cracked, arid landscape with visible riverbeds, suggesting environmental degradation. A male narrator states, "Due to positive feedback, permafrost emissions could increase the rate of warming, compounding the need for humans to reduce their emissions if climate targets are to be met." This is followed by a close-up of an oil pumpjack operating in a dry field. Hanna Lee, in a lab setting, continues, "Permafrost is actually not the largest carbon reserve on Earth. There's much larger carbon reserve in Earth's crust as fossil carbon. But we often don't talk about this carbon. This is because this carbon is considered very stable." The male narrator adds, "But permafrost carbon isn't the only potential driver of a positive feedback cycle," as a large industrial refinery with a flaring stack is shown, accompanied by mechanical whirring and clanking sounds. The segment concludes with an aerial view of a barren, brown tundra landscape with a winding stream under an overcast sky, as the ominous music continues.

event_blocks.summary: Sweeping aerial views of a vast, rugged Arctic tundra, characterized by expansive brown and yellowish vegetation, winding water channels, and scattered ponds under an overcast sky, transition to a remote campsite nestled among shrubs with colorful tents, then inside a green tent where researchers analyze data on a laptop. A narrator questions the stability of a "mega source of carbon," noting that "disturbing evidence comes from the bubbles in Easy Lake," where bubbles vigorously rise from a river surface and ascend through murky underwater depths, accompanied by distinct bubbling sounds. Scientists discovered that methane released by thawing permafrost has a particular chemical fingerprint, originating "deeper inside Earth," "much deeper," from "miles beneath the permafrost, deep in Earth's crust," where "huge fossil methane reservoirs" lie, posing the question of how this methane gets through Earth's crust. Taylor Sullivan explains that the local geology is highly fractured and faulted, and the research team walks along a marked transect across the tundra, with a satellite map highlighting 'ESIEH LAKE' and geological features, as seismic evidence suggests the area is seismically active with a network of geological fault lines, accompanied by a subtle, deep rumble sound effect. A 3D animated cross-section of the ground depicts a glowing green, oval-shaped mass beneath the surface with a crack forming above it, symbolizing how "Fault lines make cracks in Earth's crust through which fossil methane can rise to the surface." Scientists suspect a fault line lies near or directly beneath "Easy Lake," with seismic evidence suggesting it sits above 500 feet of still-frozen permafrost, trapping fossil methane, yet bubbling sounds intensify as the narrator questions how these deep stocks of greenhouse gas are breaking through. An aerial shot reveals a large, dark crater in a mossy, tundra landscape, and two researchers, one carrying a GPS device labeled 'GEN Systems' and a backpack with a blue water bladder, while the other wears a blue jacket and dark pants, navigate through low shrubs and grasses with a yellow survey tape stretched across the ground, indicating a marked research transect, as the team's sonar scan found a 50-foot hole in the lake floor. Geophysicist Nick Hasson joins the team, bringing technology used by the military, and walks through the foggy tundra, dragging a yellow survey tape across low-lying shrubs, explaining he is "scanning the permafrost using a geophysical method called very low frequency," utilizing a GEN Systems Overhauser Magnetometer on his back, which measures electromagnetic waves sent by the Navy for submarine communication, allowing him to determine if the ground is frozen by measuring the wave's speed, as "if there's permafrost or ice, these waves are coming up against a lot of resistance. But if there's no permafrost or ice, it quickly moves through."
